refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the availability target, specifies bidirectionality (command uplink + telemetry downlink), and anchors measurement to an agreed evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to loss of control.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up / total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ 0.999. Requires instrumentation of link health signals and timestamped logging.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link degradation or loss and report link state to safety services.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link availability metrics for post-evaluation analysis.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant wireless communication paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover; monitor link health continuously; report state changes to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface.
DP-B: Implement single primary wireless link with periodic health checks; escalate link loss events to Safety Services; rely on operator manual intervention for recovery.
DP-C: Implement wired tether-based command/telemetry link with wireless backup; prioritize wired path; switch to wireless on tether failure.
DP-D: Implement multi-band wireless (e.g., 4G + 5G + proprietary ISM) with load balancing and seamless handover; continuous link quality monitoring; automatic rerouting on degradation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Implement redundant wireless communication paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover; monitor link health continuously; report state changes to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface.

potential_sources: Redundancy architecture couples to vehicle power budget (dual transceivers increase power draw).
Failover logic couples to Safety Services state machine (must coordinate link state reporting with safety interlocks).
Link health monitoring couples to telemetry bandwidth (health checks consume uplink/downlink capacity).
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable via interface contracts (SafetyAlert, Telemetry). DP-A is decoupled from specific wireless technology (5G, LTE, proprietary), allowing flexibility. Redundancy is a proven pattern for availability-critical systems.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Two independent wireless paths can be provisioned at the worksite (e.g., cellular + proprietary radio).
Assumption: Automatic failover latency is acceptable to safety interlocks (typically <100 ms).
Risk: If both paths fail simultaneously (e.g., site-wide RF jamming, power loss), availability target cannot be met; mitigation requires site-level RF survey and power redundancy.
Risk: Failover logic complexity increases test burden; requires extensive scenario coverage (link flap, asymmetric loss, latency spikes).
Risk: Operator may not be aware of failover event; requires clear UI feedback (e.g., link status indicator).
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify failover latency requirement and acceptable link loss duration (e.g., max 50 ms per failover event). Define 'link operational' precisely: is it based on heartbeat timeout, signal strength threshold, or packet loss rate? Specify which wireless technologies are available at target worksites.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link, including wireless transceiver control, redundancy management, link health monitoring, and state reporting. Responsible for achieving ≥0.999 availability through DP-A (redundant paths + failover).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (e.g., drive, lift, rotate) from tele-operator console; Teleoperation module decodes and forwards to Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, link quality metrics) from Vehicle to tele-operator console; Teleoperation module aggregates and formats.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Reports link state changes (up/down/degraded) to Safety Services; triggers safety interlocks (e.g., emergency stop) on link loss.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstraction of underlying wireless infrastructure (primary + backup paths); Teleoperation module manages selection, failover, and health monitoring.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: link management, redundancy, and failover are core Teleoperation responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert reporting crosses boundary (acceptable coupling). This is the natural home for DP-A.

Poor containment: Vehicle owns physical transceivers and antennas, but link availability logic (redundancy, failover, health monitoring) is not a Vehicle concern. Assigning DP-A to Vehicle would scatter link management across Vehicle and Teleoperation, violating cohesion.

Poor containment: Safety Services owns safety interlocks and hazard response, not link infrastructure. Assigning DP-A to Safety Services would conflate link management with safety logic, creating tight coupling and test complexity.
Poor containment: NGHE Solution is the top-level system; assigning DP-A here would bypass architectural layering and prevent delegation to specialized modules.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module owns all logic and state for achieving link availability: redundant path selection, failover triggers, health monitoring, and SafetyAlert reporting. Vehicle provides physical transceiver interfaces; Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert events. This assignment maximizes cohesion and minimizes diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle module is responsible for transceiver hardware but Teleoperation owns failover logic, there is a potential ownership gap for transceiver state queries (e.g., signal strength). Mitigation: define a clear Vehicle→Teleoperation interface for transceiver health metrics.

Risk: Teleoperation module may become large if it also owns operator console UI and command parsing. Mitigation: separate UI concerns into a distinct Operator Console module; Teleoperation focuses on link and failover.

Risk: If multiple worksites use different wireless technologies (e.g., 4G vs. proprietary ISM), Teleoperation must abstract technology differences. Mitigation: use Network Link interface as abstraction; implement technology-specific drivers below.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define the Vehicle↔Teleoperation interface for transceiver control and health queries. Clarify whether Operator Console is a separate module or part of Teleoperation. Document the Network Link abstraction and supported wireless technologies.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (redundant paths + failover + health monitoring) directly addresses FR requirement for ≥0.999 availability. The DP is technology-agnostic, scalable, and proven in safety-critical systems. No logical gaps between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: Failover logic must coordinate with Safety Services (SafetyAlert interface); ensure latency and ordering are well-defined.
Transceiver control spans Vehicle (hardware) and Teleoperation (logic); define clear ownership boundary via interface contract.
Link health monitoring may require telemetry bandwidth; ensure Telemetry interface has sufficient capacity for health metrics.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) for DP-A. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal. No significant diffusion risk.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability measurement is straightforward: instrument link state (up/down) and log timestamps. Calculate availability as (time_up / total_time). Requires continuous monitoring during evaluation period; feasible with standard telemetry.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-010 is clearly allocated to Teleoperation module. Sub-FRs (010.1–010.4) map to specific DP elements: 010.1/010.2 → redundant paths, 010.3 → health monitoring + SafetyAlert, 010.4 → logging. Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Define failover latency SLA (e.g., max 50 ms) and add as acceptance criterion.
Specify link health check frequency and timeout thresholds (e.g., heartbeat every 100 ms, timeout after 3 missed beats).
Document Network Link abstraction and supported wireless technologies (e.g., 4G, 5G, proprietary ISM).
Define SafetyAlert message format and priority levels (e.g., LINK_UP, LINK_DOWN, LINK_DEGRADED).
Clarify whether Operator Console is part of Teleoperation or a separate module.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (010.1–010.4) that address distinct concerns: command uplink, telemetry downlink, link health reporting, and logging. Each sub-FR requires further refinement and DP selection. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to detail the redundancy architecture, failover logic, and health monitoring strategy.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle with latency ≤ [TBD] ms.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator with latency ≤ [TBD] ms.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link degradation or loss within [TBD] ms and report link state (UP/DOWN/DEGRADED) to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link availability metrics (timestamp, state, failover events) for post-evaluation analysis with ≥ [TBD] Hz sampling rate.

assumptions: Two independent wireless paths (primary + backup) can be provisioned at target worksites.
Automatic failover latency <100 ms is acceptable to safety interlocks.
Link state can be reliably detected via heartbeat or signal strength monitoring.
Operator console provides clear UI feedback on link status and failover events.
Evaluation period is ≥ 30 days to achieve statistically significant availability measurement.
open_risks: Simultaneous failure of both wireless paths (e.g., RF jamming, power loss) cannot be mitigated by DP-A alone; requires site-level RF survey and power redundancy.
Failover logic complexity increases test burden; extensive scenario coverage needed (link flap, asymmetric loss, latency spikes).
Operator awareness of failover events is critical for safety; UI design must be validated with operators.
Wireless technology availability varies by worksite; DP-A must be flexible to support multiple technologies (4G, 5G, proprietary ISM).
evidence_needed: Worksite RF survey and wireless technology availability (primary + backup paths).
Failover latency measurement and acceptance criteria from Safety Manager.
Link health check frequency and timeout thresholds (from reliability analysis).
Operator console UI mockup and usability testing results.
Test plan for failover scenarios (link flap, asymmetric loss, latency spikes, simultaneous failure).
Post-evaluation availability data and statistical analysis (confidence intervals).
